results.title: Significance of Healthier SG's Capitation Model on Healthcare Financing and Mechanisms to Ensure Long-term Fiscal Sustainability
results.summary: The Ministry clarified that full subsidies for preventive care under Healthier SG aim to encourage uptake among target populations, while means-tested co-payments remain for acute and chronic services to maintain fiscal discipline. This structure prevents over-servicing and cost escalation, ensuring long-term sustainability without relying on market-based price signals for demand moderation. The shift from CHAS co-payments to Healthier SG's capitation model reflects a targeted approach rather than a departure from shared responsibility principles. Specific mechanisms to prevent "buffet syndrome" were not detailed in the response.

results.title: Singapore's Stance on Fossil Fuel Phase-out Language at COP30
results.summary: Singapore supported COP28's decision on transitioning away from fossil fuels but did not endorse specific phasing-out language for COP30 due to a lack of consensus among negotiating parties. The Government remained engaged in negotiations alongside the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS) and will continue to work constructively to advance the global energy transition. No definitive position on including fossil fuel phase-out language in the COP30 decision text was stated.

results.title: Standardised Protocols for Regular Review of CCTV Footage in Government Primary Schools to Minimise Physical and Sexual Abuse of Students
results.summary: The Minister for Education stated that while schools review CCTV footage during specific incident investigations, there are no standardised protocols for regularly reviewing footage to detect abuse. This is because CCTV cameras are typically installed in high-traffic areas, making routine surveillance for potential abuse impractical. Instead, the Ministry relies on trained personnel to identify student distress, maintain open classroom doors with viewing windows, and brief coaches on professional conduct boundaries. Students from Primary 1 receive education on personal safety, boundaries, and reporting mechanisms to trusted adults. The Ministry will continue to review existing safety measures to ensure they remain effective and relevant for student well-being.

results.title: Standardising SOPs for Stroller Use across All Public Bus Operators
results.summary: The Land Transport Authority is consulting commuters and working with bus operators to review Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for stroller use, addressing cases where non-foldable strollers conflict with wheelchair bay occupancy rules. Current rules allow open strollers in unoccupied wheelchair bays but require folding if the bay is occupied or needed for a wheelchair user. A public communications campaign to educate commuters and bus captains on these SOPs is under consideration to ensure a consistent and inclusive travel experience for families across all four public bus operators.

results.title: Statistics on Retention Rates of Entry-level Care Workers and Tracking Stability of Care Industry
results.summary: Minister Ong Ye Kung reported that attrition rates for entry-level staff in Public Healthcare Institutions improved between 2022 and 2024, dropping from 14.6% to 11.5% for support care staff. While rates for doctors and nurses also declined, the Minister noted that retaining healthcare workers remains a global challenge due to high demand. The Ministry of Health maintains attractiveness of the sector through competitive remuneration, career rewards, and learning opportunities. The Minister did not provide a specific three-year retention rate figure nor confirm plans to publish new stability indicators as requested.

results.title: Support during June and Year-end Holidays for Children Without Conducive Home Environments
results.summary: Primary schools proactively identify students lacking conducive home environments or adult supervision and encourage enrolment in school-based Student Care Centres (SCCs) during June and year-end holidays. These SCCs offer academic coaching, reading support, mentoring, and activities in sports, arts, music, and coding. Additionally, primary and secondary schools partner with community agencies or vendors to provide holiday programmes including industry exposure, interest-based activities, and drop-in centres with mentoring. The Ministry of Education (MOE) does not track the proportion of students utilising these programmes in 2025. MOE will continue to resource schools adequately to support students with additional needs and collaborate with community partners to develop students holistically during school terms and holidays.

results.title: Support for Employees Affected by Abrupt Closure of Companies and Proposal to Amend Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018 to Prioritise Payout to Employees
results.summary: Employees' claims for salaries are already prioritised first under the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018, ranking above all unsecured debts except administration costs. The Ministry of Manpower investigates employers breaching legislation during winding-up and provides financial assistance via the Short-Term Relief Fund (STRF) to eligible local lower-income workers unable to receive unpaid salaries due to corporate liquidation. Between 2023 and 2025, approximately $600,000 was disbursed from the STRF to 260 affected workers. Displaced workers can access career matching services, training programmes through Workforce Singapore and SkillsFuture Singapore, and temporary financial support under the SkillsFuture Jobseeker Support scheme. The Ministry does not track the total number of workers with unpaid wages due to corporate liquidations.

results.title: Supporting SMEs and Smaller Businesses to Reduce Fire Risks at Industrial Estates
results.summary: The Minister clarified that businesses must comply with the Fire Safety Act and Workplace Safety and Health Act to ensure safety. Available resources include guidelines on flammable materials from the Workplace Safety and Health Council and fire safety advisories from the Singapore Civil Defence Force (SCDF). Companies may seek clarifications on the Fire Code via SCDF and send staff to the free Responders Plus Programme for training on fire extinguishers, CPR, and automated external defibrillators. No specific new financial support or mandatory assessment plans were announced beyond existing statutory obligations and voluntary resources.

results.title: Syariah Court Divorce Applications and Reasons for Divorce Each Year from 2021 to 2025
results.summary: Under the Administration of Muslim Law Act, applicants must complete the Marriage Counselling Programme before filing for divorce. Based on 2021 and 2024 data, the Syariah Court received an average of 2,986 MCP registrations annually, with 1,991 proceeding to divorce applications and 1,478 resulting in granted divorces. Specific breakdowns for reasons such as infidelity, financial problems, or desertion are not provided in the transcript; instead, the Acting Minister directed users to the Department of Statistics table (TS/M830267) for detailed cause-of-divorce statistics.

results.title: Strengthening Support and Dignity for Migrant Domestic Workers and Singapore Families
results.summary: The Government reaffirmed that Migrant Domestic Workers (MDWs) are the critical backbone of Singapore's care economy, noting their population grew by approximately 3% annually from 2011 to 2025. Officials clarified that care failures typically stem from broken relationships and poor matching rather than family cruelty, while current agency practices like charging extra replacement fees contribute to worker trauma. To address these issues, the Government will continue expanding caregiving options and safeguarding employer interests, with a focus on developing fit-for-purpose alternatives to live-in arrangements. Specific requests to expand training grants, professionalise agencies, extend primary healthcare coverage, integrate mental health support, create regulated part-time pathways, and review levy rules were acknowledged as part of ongoing efforts to improve employment conditions and support households facing growing care burdens.

results.title: Total Expenditure and Budgeted Fees For and Incurred for Renamed "The Kallang" and "The Kallang Group"
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Culture, Community and Youth confirmed that the rebranding of the Singapore Sports Hub to "The Kallang" and Kallang Alive Sport Management to "The Kallang Group" was executed within the existing operational budget of The Kallang Group, resulting in no increase to the overall budget provision. Since the Government took over the facility in December 2022, The Kallang has hosted over 4,000 event days and welcomed nearly 9.5 million visitors, while The Kallang Group managed more than 200 National School Games and various signature events. The destination rebranding aims to strengthen its regional reputation as a leading hub for sport, entertainment, lifestyle, and community events, leveraging planned events to ensure cost effectiveness throughout the procurement process.

results.title: Transfer of Non-pedagogical Administrative Tasks from Teachers to School Administrative and Operations Personnel
results.summary: The Ministry of Education does not mandate the transfer of specific non-pedagogical administrative tasks, such as monetary collections or event logistics, from teachers to administrative personnel. While schools are expected to utilize existing dedicated administrative teams and technology to reduce teacher workload, the Ministry allows individual school leaders flexibility in balancing duties based on their specific operating contexts and colleagues' capabilities. Consequently, there is no uniform requirement for schools to reallocate these tasks to align with workload reduction objectives.

results.title: Update on Food Security Strategy with Revised 2035 Fibre and Protein Production Targets
results.summary: The revised 2035 targets under Singapore Food Story 2 aim for local production of 20% of fresh leafy vegetables, beansprouts, and mushrooms, plus 30% of eggs and seafood, excluding meats and alternative proteins due to resource constraints. Local production serves as one pillar of food security alongside import diversification, stockpiling, and global partnerships, with rice and poultry addressed through other pillars. The Government will support farms via the Agri-Food Cluster Transformation Fund to enhance capabilities, reduce costs, and improve resilience through agri-tech and automation, rather than subsidizing operating costs or enforcing minimum sourcing quotas. Consumers and businesses are urged to support local produce to ensure commercial viability, while specific details on support schemes will be provided at the upcoming Committee of Supply.

results.title: Update on Implementation of Orchard Road Pedestrian Plans and Target Date of Completion
results.summary: The Minister for National Development confirmed that the 2022 plan to pedestrianise a 500m stretch of Orchard Road between Buyong Road and Handy Road is currently in the design phase. A specific target date for completion has not yet been announced and will be released when ready. No further timeline or status updates were provided during the session.

results.title: Update on Strengthening Consular Assistance and Crisis Response Capabilities for Singaporeans during Emergencies
results.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MFA) outlines a three-step consular response for overseas crises: assessing incident severity, disseminating information to eRegistered Singaporeans, and providing direct assistance such as issuing Documents of Identity and facilitating repatriation. During recent incidents including the Brown University, Bondi Beach, and Taipei attacks in December 2025, MFA missions immediately contacted eRegistered citizens, posted advisories, and liaised with local authorities. Specific actions included deploying a Crisis Response Team to Hat Yai floods and facilitating the departure of 11 Singaporeans from Iran in June 2025. The Minister emphasized that responses are customised to unique circumstances and urged citizens to eRegister, purchase comprehensive travel insurance, and monitor MFA Travel Advisories for safety.

results.title: Upward Trend in Verbal and Physical Abuse against Teachers in Singapore Following OECD Teaching and Learning International Survey 2024 Findings
results.summary: The Minister for Education confirmed that cases of teacher abuse by parents remain low, referencing a previous October 2025 response. To support educators, the Ministry of Education refreshed the Guidelines for School-Home Partnership in 2024, which mandate respectful communication and positive role-modelling between parents and teachers. The MOE's Engagement Charter stipulates firm actions against abusive parents, ranging from warnings and police assistance to criminal prosecution under the Protection from Harassment Act for egregious cases. While OECD TALIS 2024 data indicates a 5% average rate of verbal abuse internationally, the Minister did not confirm a specific upward trend in Singapore beyond the existing low case numbers.

results.title: Utilisation Statistics and Family Transfer Options for Unused SG Culture Pass Credits for Disabled Citizens
results.summary: As of 1 January 2026, over 1.1 million Singaporeans have registered for the SG Culture Pass, with more than $9.8 million in credits utilised by 136,000 users. The Ministry explicitly states that citizens cannot transfer unutilised credits to immediate family members; however, users may purchase tickets for others, including family, provided the total cost stays within their available balance. To enhance accessibility for citizens with disabilities or mobility challenges, the Ministry collaborates with partners like the Agency for Integrated Care to bring events to nursing homes and has included over 70 accessible cultural events since launch. Starting 1 March 2026, the scheme expands to allow the purchase of local books, enabling those with mobility issues to enjoy literary arts without travelling.

results.title: Singapore Sports Council (Amendment) Bill
results.summary: The Singapore Sports Council (Amendment) Bill seeks to update the 1973 Act to empower SportSG to lead a broader sporting ecosystem encompassing physical, mind, and e-sports. The Minister moves for the Bill's Second Reading, clarifying that SportSG will exercise leadership through industry best practices rather than acting as a regulator. While the Bill expands the Council's membership from 15 to 25 members and introduces new powers for institutional establishment, speakers note that the Council size increase is a suggestion, not a formal amendment. Debates address operational safeguards for e-sports, including youth well-being and screen time, alongside concerns on funding balance and potential conflicts of interest regarding equipment manufacturing. The Ministry is requested to provide further clarifications on membership rationale, scholarship criteria, and facility access processes to ensure the expansion complements traditional sports without diluting resources.
